loop_mechanisms.immunometabolic: Activated immune cells accumulate succinate, which stabilizes HIF-1α and drives IL-1β production — the pro-inflammatory arm of this hub. Itaconate, produced via the same IRG1/ACOD1 pathway, runs in the opposite direction: it inhibits succinate dehydrogenase and is anti-inflammatory in the cell-biology literature. It is not the driver of inflammation here — succinate is. Whether this specific succinate/itaconate shunt operates as a driver of the low-energy state in Long COVID or ME/CFS has not been directly confirmed in either disease; it remains an extrapolation from general immunometabolism, popularized as a hypothesis by ME/CFS researchers (the Open Medicine Foundation's ‘itaconate shunt’ proposal) but without a disease-specific primary study demonstrating it operates in patients.
loop_mechanisms.redox_inflammation: The proposed loop: electron transport chain dysfunction causes electron leak and reactive oxygen species production, which damages cardiolipin, respiratory complexes, and mtDNA, while also activating NF-κB-driven cytokine release — with glutathione and NAD⁺ consumed defending against the resulting oxidative load. This is a coherent general redox-biology model. Direct disease-specific confirmation is thin and genuinely mixed: peripheral oxidative-stress markers have been reported elevated in some ME/CFS cohorts, but the one central-nervous-system measurement identified in this review — a direct brain glutathione measurement — did not find the reduction the model predicts.
loop_mechanisms.nad_depletion: Chronic inflammation upregulates the NAD⁺-consuming enzyme CD38, and DNA/oxidative damage activates PARP enzymes, both of which drain cellular NAD⁺. Falling NAD⁺ impairs oxidative phosphorylation and disables the sirtuin→PGC-1α mitochondrial biogenesis pathway — the repair loop that would otherwise restore energy capacity. This is the clearest example on this page of a restorative loop being actively disabled rather than merely absent. Both halves of the general mechanism (CD38 induction, NAD⁺'s role in biogenesis) are established in aging/general cell biology. Direct evidence that NAD⁺ is depleted at baseline in Long COVID or ME/CFS patients specifically was not found in this review — what exists are trials measuring NAD⁺ as an outcome of supplementation, which is evidence about treatment response, not about baseline pathology.
loop_mechanisms.vascular_o2: Endothelial dysfunction, fibrinaloid microclots, and platelet activation are proposed to impair peripheral oxygen delivery and extraction, driving local tissue hypoxia that feeds back into HIF stabilization and further endothelial injury. Invasive cardiopulmonary exercise testing has directly documented a preload-failure-plus-impaired-peripheral-extraction phenotype in a subset of ME/CFS patients. The microclot/platelet component of this loop is more contested: no completed randomized trial of anticoagulant or apheresis treatment based on this hypothesis was identified, and it remains an actively debated addition to the vascular story rather than a settled one.
loop_mechanisms.autonomic: Sympathetic overdrive and vagal withdrawal are proposed to drive orthostatic hypoperfusion (including POTS) and a loss of the cholinergic anti-inflammatory reflex — a vagally-mediated brake on systemic inflammation. GPCR-targeting autoantibodies (beta-adrenergic, muscarinic) have been proposed as a candidate node linking autonomic dysfunction to the inflammatory loops elsewhere on this page. This is a genuinely contested finding: one Charité-group study found elevated GPCR autoantibody reactivity in a CFS subset, while a larger 2025 replication using different profiling technology found no significant difference from controls.
loop_mechanisms.neuroinflammatory: Peripheral inflammatory signals are proposed to activate central microglia, producing sickness-behavior-like effects and altering the brain's effort-cost computations in a way that reduces activity — with primed microglia potentially lowering the threshold for the next flare. Direct imaging evidence in ME/CFS is limited to one small PET study; it should be read as a preliminary signal, not a replicated finding.
loop_mechanisms.pem_recovery: Exertion is proposed to trigger a non-resolving immune/metabolic flare, such that physiological reserve fails to replenish before the next exertional demand, starting the system from a deficit each time — with deconditioning layered on top as a secondary consequence, not the primary lesion. The signature empirical test is two-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET): whether VO₂peak or the ventilatory/anaerobic threshold falls on a second test performed roughly 24 hours after the first. This is a genuinely contested area of the literature: several groups report a reproducible day-2 decline, while the largest and most recent replication found no such effect.

claims.statement: The IDO Metabolic Trap hypothesis proposes that substrate inhibition of IDO1 creates a kinetically bistable tryptophan-metabolism switch, gated by IDO2 genetic variants, that could lock the system into an altered kynurenine-pathway state.
